submission.raw_text:
My name is Alex Rivera and I’m a freelance web designer. In early April I agreed to build a five-page marketing website for Maya Chen’s consulting business for $2,400. She paid a $1,200 deposit, with the balance due when the project was completed. The signed agreement is attached.

The intended launch was around May 20, although the contract also says the timeline depended on Maya supplying final copy and images by April 25. She did not send everything by then. I think the last major batch arrived around May 8 or May 9, but some small text changes kept coming after that.

The original job was a homepage, about page, services page, contact page, and mobile-responsive layout, with two revision rounds. During the project Maya also asked for a pricing comparison section, a newsletter signup, and several changes to the homepage design. I accepted most of those requests through WhatsApp because I wanted to keep the client happy. I probably should have documented more clearly which requests were outside the original scope.

I sent what I considered a complete staging version on June 3. Maya replied that it “looked really good overall” but gave me a list of changes. I made most of them during the following week. She later said the project was too late, that the mobile version had major problems, and that the delay had damaged a marketing campaign she planned to run.

There were some mobile issues, but I do not agree that the website was unusable. I fixed the issues I could reproduce. Maya also used images from the website in social media posts, and I believe at least part of the site was briefly published, although I cannot prove exactly what was live or for how long.

Maya now refuses to pay the remaining $1,200 and has also said she may ask for the deposit back. She says I disappeared for two weeks. That is exaggerated. I was slower for part of one week because of a family issue, but I still replied to messages and told her what was happening.

I feel like she is using the missed target date as an excuse not to pay for work she received. I am asking for the remaining $1,200. I will transfer the final editable source files and administrator credentials when the balance is paid. I want JuryAI to consider that much of the schedule delay came from late content, added requests, and repeated changes.

Alex states that the intended launch was around May 20 and that the timeline depended on Maya supplying final copy and images by April 25.

open_issues: The signed agreement has not been inspected, so its exact wording and any additional terms remain unverified.
It is unclear whether May 20 was a binding deadline or an approximate launch target.
It is unclear how completion was defined and whether payment was contingent on acceptance, publication, or correction of defects.
It is unclear whether the additional requests were formally added to scope or price.
The basis for conditioning delivery of source files and credentials on payment has not been established from the uninspected agreement.

claim_evidence_links.explanation: Alex identifies the signed agreement as the source of the project price, deposit, and payment trigger.
Alex states that the contract contains the intended launch timing and client-content dependency.
The described messages could establish when content and later text changes were supplied.
The described WhatsApp messages could show the additional requests and their timing.
The WhatsApp history could clarify which additional requests Alex accepted and whether scope treatment was discussed.
The staging deliverable could show its condition and completeness as of June 3.
The described feedback message allegedly contains the quoted positive assessment and requested changes.
Inspection and testing of the relevant website version would be needed to assess the alleged mobile issues and usability.
The described social media posts could establish use of website images.
The described communications could establish refusal to pay the $1,200 balance and the possible deposit demand.
A complete message history could establish the duration and frequency of Alex's communications.
The described messages could establish the timing of client content, additional requests, and repeated changes.
The described communication is the stated source of Maya's alleged campaign-damage assertion.

damages_claims.causal_theory: Alex states that the agreed $2,400 project price included a $1,200 balance due upon completion and that Maya refuses to pay that balance despite receiving the work.
damages_claims.calculation_basis: $2,400 total price minus the $1,200 deposit already paid.

required_actions: After receiving the $1,200 balance, Alex will transfer the final editable source files and administrator credentials to Maya.
outcomes.rationale: Alex seeks the unpaid contract balance and states that the final editable files and administrator credentials will be transferred when payment is made.

clarification_questions.question: What year did the project occur?
clarification_questions.reason: The narrative gives months and days but does not identify the year.

clarification_questions.question: What exact language does the signed agreement use for the May 20 launch, the April 25 content dependency, completion, acceptance, final payment, and transfer of source files or credentials?
clarification_questions.reason: These terms determine the asserted deadline, payment trigger, and remaining delivery obligations, but the agreement has not been inspected.

clarification_questions.question: Which specific copy and images were still missing on April 25, when was each item supplied, and can the relevant dated messages be identified?
clarification_questions.reason: This would clarify the extent and timing of the claimed client-content delay.

clarification_questions.question: Which additional requests did you complete, which did you decline or leave unfinished, and did the WhatsApp messages discuss added time, added price, or a revised launch date?
clarification_questions.reason: The narrative does not fully establish the scope or schedule treatment of the added requests.

clarification_questions.question: What work remained after the June 3 staging delivery and after the following week's revisions, including any unresolved mobile issues?
clarification_questions.reason: This is needed to assess Alex's asserted completion and the condition of the deliverable when payment was requested.

clarification_questions.question: What dates or message records show your communications during the period Maya describes as a two-week disappearance?
clarification_questions.reason: A complete communication timeline could clarify the duration and significance of the alleged communication gap.

clarification_questions.question: When did you first request the remaining $1,200, and what response did Maya give about why she would not pay?
clarification_questions.reason: This would establish the payment-demand timeline and the stated basis for withholding payment.
